sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Pulmonary embolism (PE) is a life-threatening condition caused by obstruction of the pulmonary arteries by thrombi, commonly originating from deep vein thrombosis (DVT). Early recognition and prompt management can significantly reduce mortality and morbidity.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Acute onset dyspnoea (shortness of breath), often severe and unexplained
- Pleuritic chest pain (sharp, worse on breathing in)
- Tachypnoea (rapid breathing)
- Tachycardia (fast heart rate)
- Syncope or near-syncope
- Haemoptysis (coughing blood; less common but significant)
- Hypoxia (altered oxygen saturation, cyanosis)
- Signs of deep vein thrombosis (DVT), i.e., unilateral leg swelling, pain, redness, localised warmth
- Sudden hypotension/shock: indicating massive PE
- Anxiety or sense of impending doom
- Low-grade fever (occasionally)
- Jugular venous distension
- ECG changes: Sinus tachycardia (most common), (incomplete) right bundle branch block, right ventricular strain pattern, right axis deviation, right atrial enlargement (P-Pulmonale), S1Q3T3, clockwise rotation (late transition zone), atrial fibrillation/flutter

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Previous history of DVT, PE or cancer
- Recent long-haul flights or prolonged periods of immobilisation
- Currently pregnant or post-partum
- Recent trauma or surgery
- Oral contraceptive or oestrogen use
- NSAIDs are generally avoided in PE due to bleeding risk and lack of acute therapeutic benefit
- Suspect PE with chest pain plus dyspnoea, tachycardia, syncope, new hypoxia, haemoptysis, or unilateral leg swelling suggestive of DVT
- Differentials may include myocardial infarction, pneumonia, pericarditis, pleurisy, pneumothorax, pericardial tamponade, or acute heart failure
- Risk of sudden deterioration: unstable PE may rapidly progress to cardiac arrest
- A 12 lead ECG can show changes related to PE, but a normal ECG does not exclude it

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey
- Prioritise airway, breathing, and circulation
- Oxygen only if required, titrated to clinical need
- Position patient to optimise breathing, usually sitting upright if tolerated
- Perform regular vital signs and frequent reassessment
- Provide appropriate analgesia if required
- Obtain ECG monitoring where available
- Consider Secondary Survey

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Gain IV access
- Perform 12 lead ECG
- Consider small fluid boluses only if hypotensive and reassess frequently, especially if right ventricular strain is suspected
- Consider stronger analgesia if required
- Consider antiemetic if required
- Anticoagulation decisions should be made under local protocol and senior medical direction rather than used routinely from this guideline alone
- Escalate early for hypotension, syncope, marked hypoxia, or suspected massive PE

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- D-dimer, formal risk scores, and definitive imaging are hospital decisions and should not delay transport from a prehospital guideline
- A normal ECG does not exclude PE
- Sinus tachycardia is common, but ECG changes are neither sensitive nor specific enough to rule PE in or out on their own
- Aspirin has no established role in the immediate treatment of acute PE
